text: Laparoscopic right and transverse colectomy with radical mesocolic excision and intracorporeal anastomosis for obstructing transverse colon carcinoma. A seventy-three year old gentleman presents to ED complaining of colicky abdominal pain, nausea and inability to pass stools for several days. Abdominal CT scan shows a subacute large bowel obstruction from a transverse colon mass associated with mild distension of the distal small bowel loops.
An urgent colonoscopy confirms the malignant stricture at the level of the mid-transverse colon. The stricture cannot be traversed by the scope and any attempt at stenting fails.

The surgeon performs an emergency laparoscopic operation, establishing pneumoperitoneum after optical blunt entrance in the left upper quadrant. Four additional blunt ports are inserted under visual guidance in the left flank, left iliac fossa, right flank, and right iliac fossa.

During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies the transverse colon cancer. The right colon proximal to the stricture and the transverse and descending colon distal to the stricture appear visibly dilated. The small bowel shows signs of distention due to the malignant obstruction and previous stenting attempts.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic right and transverse colectomy, beginning with the identification and clipping of the iliocolic vessels at their origin from the superior mesenteric vessels using a harmonic scalpel. The iliocolic vessels are then sectioned separately.

The surgeon performs a radical right medial to lateral mesocolic excision, following avascular planes. The duodenum is gently dissected from the mesocolon, with careful visualization and preservation of the right ureter in the retroperitoneum, ensuring Tolt's fascia remains intact.

The surgeon uses an intercorporeal stapler to divide the terminal ileal loop. The right and middle colic vessels are carefully identified, clipped, and sealed separately at their origin near the top of the pancreas.

In highly selected cases, with an experienced surgeon with advanced laparoscopic colorectal skills, emergency laparoscopic radical resection and primary intracorporeal anastomosis for obstructing colonic cancer is safe, feasible and allows remarkable advantages. in terms of avoiding stoma formation, faster recovery, less pain, less lung infections, and lower rates of incisional hernias and surgical site infections.

The surgeon performs a challenging dissection of the transverse mesocolon, carefully navigating the short distance from the duodenum and the lower edge of the pancreas. The dissection follows avascular planes to prevent iatrogenic injury to the pancreatic gland.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colic gutter and takes down the hepatic flexure, exposing the gastrocolic ligament and opening the lesser sac. The splenic flexure is also completely taken down to ensure well-perfused colonic tissue, despite the presence of bowel obstruction.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the distal portion of the colon, completing the resection with precise cutting and minimal tissue trauma.

The surgeon resects the colon at the proximal descending colon using an intracorporeal stapler. The freed specimen is extracted through a six-centimeter mini fan and steel incision with a wound protector. The bowel ends, appearing well-perfused, are approximated without tension for a primary intracorporeal anastomosis. The ileal stump is retrieved and correctly oriented, and the bowel stumps are approximated with stay sutures.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to incise the serosal and muscular layers of the enterotomies, while a blunt dissector is employed to open the mucosa, preventing damage to the opposite side of the bowel. An intracorporeal side-to-side and isoperistaltic ileodescending colon anastomosis is performed, followed by closing the enterotomy with a hand-sewn double-layer suture.

The assistant holds the stay suture to elevate the edge of the enterotomy, clearly displaying the margins of the incision. This maneuver helps prevent enteric spillage from the proximal bowel during the intracorporeal anastomosis.
The patient is alive with no evidence of disease at one year follow-up.
